evidence_quotes.quote: The video game market is especially poisoned with this malpractice, where countless games purchased in the belief that they will be operable in some form in the future simply get shut down with no possible way of even continuing the service on your own terms.
predatory "monthly" subscriptions that will incur hefty "cancellation fees" if you do not wish to use the software anymore
"lifetime" purchased products unavailable at the mercy of the software publisher.

paragraphs.text: I believe that this motion should include provisions that extend to digital software licenses, especially for stopping the unfortunately common practice of making supposedly "lifetime" purchased products unavailable at the mercy of the software publisher. The video game market is especially poisoned with this malpractice, where countless games purchased in the belief that they will be operable in some form in the future simply get shut down with no possible way of even continuing the service on your own terms. The likely most notable/already well known and documented example from other EU proceedings would be The Crew by Ubisoft, a French game development company. Other digital licenses like the Adobe Creative Cloud and purchases of now considered "legacy" versions of lifetime licenses to Photoshop CS6 are no longer available, with the company now forcing EU citizens to sign up for predatory "monthly" subscriptions that will incur hefty "cancellation fees" if you do not wish to use the software anymore while making the legacy versions no longer available to download or use. I'm hopeful that the digital fairness act will be able to reign in the unfortunately often extremely consumer-hostile tech companies substantially, given such changes.
